1.o: Faith in Christ, and love to him, should unite saints more closely than any outward relation can unite the people of the world. Paul in his private prayers was particular in remembering his friends. We must remember Christian friends much and often, as their cases may need, bearing them in our thoughts, and upon our hearts, before our God. Different sentiments and ways in what is not essential, must not make difference of affection, as to the truth. He inquired concerning his friends, as to the truth, growth, and fruitfulness of their graces, their faith in Christ, and love to him, and to all the saints. The good which Philemon did, was matter of joy and comfort to him and others, who therefore desired that he would continue and abound in good fruits, more and more, to God's honour.
1.m:
Having faith in Christ and loving him should bring believers closer together than any family ties do for regular people. Paul made sure to pray about his friends regularly. We should think about our Christian friends often, especially if they need support. Small differences in beliefs or practices shouldn’t affect how we care for each other. 

Paul asked about his friends’ spiritual growth—how strong their faith in Christ and love for him and other believers was. When Philemon did good things, it brought joy to Paul and others. They hoped Philemon would keep growing and doing even more good things to honor God.

1.o: It does not lower any one to condescend, and sometimes even to beseech, where, in strictness of right, we might command: the apostle argues from love, rather than authority, in behalf of one converted through his means; and this was Onesimus. In allusion to that name, which signifies “profitable,” the apostle allows that in time past he had been unprofitable to Philemon, but hastens to mention the change by which he had become profitable. Unholy persons are unprofitable; they answer not the great end of their being. But what happy changes conversion makes! of evil, good; of unprofitable, useful. Religious servants are treasures in a family. Such will make conscience of their time and trusts, and manage all they can for the best. No prospect of usefulness should lead any to neglect their obligations, or to fail in obedience to superiors. One great evidence of true repentance consists in returning to practise the duties which have been neglected. In his unconverted state, Onesimus had withdrawn, to his master's injury; but now he had seen his sin and repented, he was willing and desirous to return to his duty. Little do men know for what purposes the Lord leaves some to change their situations, or engage in undertakings, perhaps from evil motives. Had not the Lord overruled some of our ungodly projects, we may reflect upon cases, in which our destruction must have been sure.
1.m:
It doesn't hurt anyone to ask nicely instead of giving orders. The apostle Paul used love instead of authority to talk about Onesimus, who became a Christian because of him. 

Onesimus’s name means “useful.” Paul said that before, Onesimus wasn’t helpful to his owner Philemon, but now he has changed and is useful. People who don’t follow God aren’t helpful—they don’t serve the purpose they were meant to. But becoming a Christian changes everything. It turns bad people into good ones and useless people into useful ones.

Christian servants are valuable in a household. They care about their work and try to help everyone. We shouldn’t think about being useful if it means ignoring our responsibilities or disobeying those in charge. A sign of real regret is fixing the wrongs you did before.

When Onesimus hadn’t believed yet, he caused trouble for his owner. Now that he sees his mistakes, he wants to go back and be responsible again. Sometimes God lets things happen that seem bad at first, so He can use them later for good. Without God’s control, some of our selfish plans could cause us harm.

1.o: When we speak of the nature of any sin or offence against God, the evil of it is not to be lessened; but in a penitent sinner, as God covers it, so must we. Such changed characters often become a blessing to all among whom they reside. Christianity does not do away our duties to others, but directs to the right doing of them. True penitents will be open in owning their faults, as doubtless Onesimus had been to Paul, upon his being awakened and brought to repentance; especially in cases of injury done to others. The communion of saints does not destroy distinction of property. This passage is an instance of that being imputed to one, which is contracted by another; and of one becoming answerable for another, by a voluntary engagement, that he might be freed from the punishment due to his crimes, according to the doctrine that Christ of his own will bore the punishment of our sins, that we might receive the reward of his righteousness. Philemon was Paul's son in the faith, yet he entreated him as a brother. Onesimus was a poor slave, yet Paul besought for him as if seeking some great thing for himself. Christians should do what may give joy to the hearts of one another. From the world they expect trouble; they should find comfort and joy in one another. When any of our mercies are taken away, our trust and hope must be in God. We must diligently use the means, and if no other should be at hand, abound in prayer. Yet, though prayer prevails, it does not merit the things obtained. And if Christians do not meet on earth, still the grace of the Lord Jesus will be with their spirits, and they will soon meet before the throne to join for ever in admiring the riches of redeeming love. The example of Onesimus may encourage the vilest sinners to return to God, but it is shamefully prevented, if any are made bold thereby to persist in evil courses. Are not many taken away in their sins, while others become more hardened? Resist not present convictions, lest they return no more.
1.m:
When talking about any sin or wrong against God, we shouldn’t downplay its seriousness. But when someone truly repents, just as God forgives them, we should too. People who turn over a new leaf often end up helping those around them.

Christianity doesn't cancel out our duty to others. It teaches us how to treat people properly. Those who really feel sorry for their mistakes will openly admit their faults. For example, Onesimus admitted his wrongdoing to Paul after he became sorry for what he did.

The belongings of believers belong to each person individually, even within the church community. Here, we see one person taking responsibility for another’s actions voluntarily. This shows how Jesus took the punishment for our sins so we could have his righteousness.

Philemon was like Paul’s spiritual child, yet Paul treated him as a brother. Onesimus was a lowly slave, but Paul asked for mercy on his behalf as if asking for something important for himself. Christians should try to bring joy to each other’s lives. In the world, we face troubles, but we should find happiness and joy in each other.

If we lose something good, we need to put our trust in God. We should work hard using whatever methods we have, including praying a lot. Even if our prayers get answered, we don’t earn the blessings ourselves.

Even if believers aren’t together on Earth, the kindness of Jesus will be with them spiritually. They’ll eventually meet forever in heaven to praise God’s love.

Onesimus’ story encourages even the worst sinners to come back to God. But it would be shameful if anyone used this story to keep doing bad things. Many people die in their sins, while others become harder-hearted. Don’t ignore the guilt you feel now, because it might not come again later.

1.o: Never have believers found more enjoyment of God, than when suffering together for him. Grace is the best wish for ourselves and others; with this the apostle begins and ends. All grace is from Christ; he purchased, and he bestows it. What need we more to make us happy, than to have the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ with our spirit? Let us do that now, which we should do at the last breath. Then men are ready to renounce the world, and to prefer the least portion of grace and faith before a kingdom.
1.m: Believers find the most joy in God when they suffer together for him. The best wish for ourselves and others is to have God’s grace. All grace comes from Christ—he bought it and gives it out. What more do we need to be happy than to have the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ in our lives? We should do what we will do at our last breath—reject the world and choose even the smallest bit of grace and faith over a whole kingdom.
